      Wells Fargo FX
   =20
 =20
There is at this point a standard way for a bank to rip off its foreign-exc=
hange customers. It goes like this. A customer =E2=80=94=C2=A0a corporate c=
lient, small business, small bank, whatever =E2=80=94=C2=A0wants to use a b=
ank for regular foreign-exchange transactions; its customers pay in euros a=
nd it wants dollars, or it has dollars and its suppliers want to be paid in=
 euros. The bank says, sure, we will change dollars into euros for you when=
ever you like, and charge you 25 pips. If you want euros and we can buy the=
m for $1.1600, we=E2=80=99ll sell them to you for $1.1625. The customer and=
 the bank agree to this pricing.Then the customer comes to the bank and say=
s =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99d like 10 million euros please.=E2=80=9D The bank says=
, sure, we=E2=80=99ll have them by the end of the day. At the end of the da=
y the bank looks at the trading range of the euro. Say the euro traded as l=
ow as $1.1685 and as high as $1.1732 that day. The bank will just=C2=A0pick=
 the highest price, and charge the customer that, plus the (agreed) markup.=
 So the customer will pay $1.1732 (the high price) plus $0.0025 (the markup=
), or $1.1757 total, or $11,757,000 for its 10 million euros. The customer =
thinks the bank made $25,000 (the 25-pip markup on 10 million euros). Of co=
urse the bank didn=E2=80=99t actually pay the highest price. If it bought t=
he euros at, say, $1.1711, then it made $46,000 on the trade.[1] It has alm=
ost doubled its fee without telling the customer.This is bad! At least, it =
is bad if the bank has said things to the client like =E2=80=9Cwe will try =
to get you the best price=E2=80=9D rather than =E2=80=9Cwe will try to get =
you the worst price, to maximize our markup.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0That is common, =
and so banks have gotten in trouble for it. (We  talked about Bank of New Y=
ork Mellon getting in trouble for it in 2015.) It=E2=80=99s a thing, it hap=
pens, it=E2=80=99s bad, it=E2=80=99s a standard way to get in trouble.Once =
you are doing it, though =E2=80=A6=C2=A0I mean, there are two ways to think=
 about this trade. One is =E2=80=9Cwell, we=C2=A0could have=C2=A0bought eur=
os at the highest price, so we=E2=80=99re not being particularly dishonest =
by charging them the highest price.=E2=80=9D This does not seem especially =
compelling. The other is =E2=80=9Cwell, the customer=C2=A0isn=E2=80=99t pay=
ing attention, so we might as well charge them the highest price. We are ex=
perts in the FX market, and they aren=E2=80=99t, so let=E2=80=99s use our e=
xpertise to gouge them.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0If that=E2=80=99s how you think about=
 the trade, it seems silly to limit yourself to the highest trading price o=
f the day. If the client is paying a little bit of attention, you might foo=
l her by charging the highest price of the day. But if she=E2=80=99s not pa=
ying attention at all, why not charge her more? Why not just=C2=A0make up=
=C2=A0a number and see what happens?=C2=A0Today federal prosecutors announc=
ed a $72.6 million settlement with Wells Fargo Bank N.A. for overcharging b=
usiness FX customers. Wells Fargo definitely did the standard, highest-pric=
e-of-the-day thing:Furthermore, rather than charging the agreed-upon fixed =
spread to the FX market rate at the time the outgoing wire was converted, F=
X sales specialists would select the best rate for the Bank and worst rate =
for the customer from the FX price fluctuations from the beginning of the t=
rading day until the time of the transaction. =C2=A0This practice was refer=
red to internally as =E2=80=9CRange of Day=E2=80=9D Pricing.And here is how=
=C2=A0the complaint=C2=A0describes Wells Fargo=E2=80=99s pricing when it co=
nverted incoming wire transfers for customers:Because Wells Fargo generally=
 did not provide immediate notice to customers when they received incoming =
wires, known as BSwifts, Wells Fargo's FX sales=C2=A0specialists took advan=
tage of this time delay to charge higher spreads than the Bank had represen=
ted it would.=C2=A0One method used by FX sales specialists to extract large=
 sales margins on BSwift transactions was to price them based on the range =
of the day. This practice allowed FX sales=C2=A0specialists to generate eve=
n larger spreads on incoming wire transactions than on outgoing wires. With=
 outgoing wires, FX sales specialists could select the best price for the B=
ank and the worst price for the customer only from the beginning of the tra=
ding day up until the time of the transaction. However, with BSwift wires, =
because the customers generally did not know when=C2=A0the funds arrived in=
 their accounts or when they were converted, the FX sales specialist could =
wait until the end of the day and cherry-pick the best rate for the Bank an=
d the worst rate for the customer from the entire trading day.In a written =
instant message to a colleague, an FX sales specialist analogized this frau=
dulent scheme to getting candy from a pinata, referring to the Bank's prici=
ng of BSwift wire transfers as the "BSWIFT pinata."=C2=A0Fine sure right. B=
ut that is just a jumping-off point; once you=E2=80=99re making up prices, =
why not, you know, really make up prices?=C2=A0For some customers, FX sales=
 specialists also used what they internally called the "big figure trick" o=
r the "transposition error game" to increase the FX sales margin and their =
profits. To carry out this scheme, the FX specialist would intentionally tr=
anspose the digits in the price of the transactions in a way that would res=
ult in the customers paying Wells Fargo a higher spread or sales margin.For=
 example, hypothetically, if the actual correct price to purchase a Euro wa=
s 1.0123 dollars, an FX sales specialist would use the big figure trick to =
switch the price to 1.0213 dollars, thus taking more spread (in this exampl=
e, an additional 89 basis points) from the customer.If the customer actuall=
y caught the error, the FX sales specialist would falsely claim that it was=
 simply a mistake.One FX sales specialist explained in an internal communic=
ation with colleagues: "You can play the transposition error game if you ge=
t called out." Another FX sales specialist noted to a colleague regarding a=
 previous transaction that a customer "didn't flinch at the big fig the oth=
er day. Want to take a bit more?"Man. I love writing about the complex stra=
tagems that the world=E2=80=99s biggest and most sophisticated financial in=
stitutions use to give themselves an edge. My favorite remains =E2=80=9Cif =
someone sends you money by accident,  keep it,=E2=80=9D but this one is pre=
tty good too. =E2=80=9CSwitch the digits in a price to make it higher, and =
hope the customer doesn=E2=80=99t notice,=E2=80=9D that=E2=80=99s high fina=
nce right there.

What=C2=A0is=C2=A0a special purpose acquisition company? Here is the story =
that I would have told you a year ago, at the height of the SPAC boom. A SP=
AC is a pool of money that a sponsor raises to take a private company publi=
c. The sponsor sells stock in the SPAC at $10 per share=C2=A0(say she sells=
 20 million shares for $200 million), puts the money in a pot,=C2=A0and goe=
s out looking for a private company. When she finds a promising private com=
pany, the sponsor says, look, I=E2=80=99ve got $200 million in my pot,=C2=
=A0and=C2=A0the pot is a publicly listed stock; if you merge with my pot, y=
ou will get the money to run your business, and you will have a public list=
ing.The private company will find this appealing. For one thing, it is a fa=
ster and easier route to a public listing than a traditional initial public=
 offering: Merging with an existing public company (the SPAC) is a bit quic=
ker and more reliable than filing your own prospectus to go public, and you=
 have a  bit more leeway in how you market your stock if you=E2=80=99re doi=
ng a merger rather than an IPO.For another thing, it offers the private com=
pany much more certainty of price and size: With a SPAC deal, the private c=
ompany and the sponsor agree on a valuation, and then the sponsor hands ove=
r the pot of money at that valuation. Instead of launching an IPO and hopin=
g to be able to raise $200 million at a price you like, you can agree on th=
e price and the $200 million with the SPAC sponsor in advance; then you ann=
ounce the deal with the money already there.Investors in the SPAC also find=
 this appealing: It is, in effect, a way to invest in a company as it goes =
public, alongside a smart investor (the SPAC sponsor) at the going-public p=
rice. Normally, IPOs are allocated to big institutional investors; ordinary=
 retail investors can=E2=80=99t buy newly public stocks until they start tr=
ading on the exchange, usually at a much higher price (the =E2=80=9CIPO pop=
=E2=80=9D). With a SPAC, if you put your $10 into the pot, and the SPAC ann=
ounces a good deal, the SPAC will trade up, and you will benefit from  the =
SPAC pop.=C2=A0This story was not wrong, really, and it accurately captured=
 a lot of the dynamics of the SPAC market in late 2020 and early 2021. It e=
lides certain details. I didn=E2=80=99t mention that SPACs offer their inve=
stors withdrawal rights: If you don=E2=80=99t like the deal that a SPAC doe=
s, you can get your $10 back, with a bit of interest. That undermines the p=
rice-and-size-certainty argument for SPACs, but it didn=E2=80=99t matter al=
l that much because, at the time, SPAC deals were hot, they traded up on an=
nouncement, and so no one had much reason to withdraw their money.I would n=
o longer tell you this story. Now here is how a SPAC (sometimes)=C2=A0works=
:=09A sponsor raises money from investors at $10 a share. Say she raises $2=
00 million by selling 20 million shares.=09The sponsor finds a private comp=
any to merge with and agrees on a price. Say they value the private company=
 at $800 million pre-money; the SPAC=E2=80=99s $200 million will buy 20% of=
 the combined company.=09The sponsor and the private company go out and fin=
d PIPE investors (=E2=80=9Cprivate investment in public equity=E2=80=9D), i=
nstitutional investors (including perhaps=C2=A0the private company=E2=80=99=
s existing investors, or the SPAC sponsor=E2=80=99s own investment funds) t=
hat want to invest in the private company in the SPAC merger. Say they rais=
e $150 million of PIPE money.=09The sponsor and the private company announc=
e their merger.=09The SPAC investors take all their money back. They get ba=
ck, like, $10.01 per share, with the extra penny being interest that the SP=
AC earned on their money. Actually I exaggerate; really only 95% of the SPA=
C investors take their money back. There is $10 million left in the SPAC po=
ol.=09The SPAC and the private company merge. The combined company has $160=
 million of cash, $150 million from the PIPE deal and $10 million from what=
=E2=80=99s left in the SPAC pool.=09The combined company is also public, wi=
th a valuation of about $960 million ($800 million pre-money valuation plus=
 $160 million of new cash) and a public=C2=A0float of about $10 million.Tha=
t=E2=80=99s =E2=80=A6=C2=A0that=E2=80=99s weird, right? The Financial Times=
 reports:Investors are pulling cash out of special purpose acquisition comp=
anies at increasingly higher rates, with a number of vehicles having their =
trust accounts almost wiped out as more than 90 per cent of their sharehold=
ers redeemed investments.The average redemption rate during the third quart=
er was 52.4 per cent, according to data provider Dealogic. =E2=80=A6The tre=
nd casts doubt over claims that listing through a Spac offers greater deal =
certainty than a traditional initial public offering, one of the key benefi=
ts touted by proponents.=C2=A0I have  argued before that in some sense the =
platonic form of a SPAC is to get rid of the pool of cash. Without the pool=
 of cash, a SPAC deal looks more or less like (1) you raise a final private=
 investment round (from the PIPE investors) and then (2) you do a direct li=
sting so your stock can trade on the stock exchange. With some SPACs it doe=
s seem like a direct listing would have been easier:Biopharmaceutical start=
-up eFFECTOR Therapeutics expected to receive at least $100m in proceeds fr=
om its merger with Locust Walk Acquisition, a Spac that raised $175m when i=
t listed in January. However, the cash held in the trust account was almost=
 entirely wiped out when 97 per cent of shareholders chose to redeem, leavi=
ng just $5.2m.=C2=A0While some of the shortfall was covered by a $60m priva=
te investment in public equity transaction, eFFECTOR received just $53.5m a=
fter fees and expenses.=C2=A0It got $5.2 million of public money and $60 mi=
llion of private money, though about two-thirds of that came from its exist=
ing investors. Its capital structure barely budged; the SPAC did nothing fo=
r it as a fundraising tool. But it is public now; that=E2=80=99s something.=
But here=E2=80=99s the really weird thing about these busted SPACs. Why wou=
ld investors withdraw 97% of their money from a SPAC? Presumably the answer=
 is that they think the merger deal is worth less than $10 per share. Given=
 the choice of a share of eFFECTOR Therapeutics stock or $10 in cash, 97% o=
f Locust Walk shareholders chose the $10.You might think that, after that, =
the remaining shares might trade down: The market voted that they were wort=
h less than $10, so presumably they=E2=80=99d trade for less than $10. Or m=
aybe they=E2=80=99d trade at $10, because the people who chose to keep them=
 think they=E2=80=99re worth $10 (and declined the cash). Or maybe $10.20, =
for scarcity value or something.=C2=A0And in fact, in the days leading up t=
o the closing of the merger and the redemption of 97% of Locust Walk=E2=80=
=99s stock, the shares traded below $10. And then the merger closed on Aug.=
 25 and the stock went to $16.98. Weird! By Sept. 3 it was back down to $8.=
56. By Sept. 13 it was at $26.73. It closed yesterday at $16.89.=C2=A0The m=
ain point to make here, I think, is that you have a company with a market c=
apitalization of more than $600 million, very little of which trades. The d=
ay after the merger closed, the stock available to trade was basically the =
$5.2 million worth of SPAC stock that had not redeemed. A tiny float makes =
for volatile trading.This is actually a common SPAC story now. The Wall Str=
eet Journal has a story about all the SPACs that have had heavy redemptions=
 followed by wild price rallies:Day traders are targeting some companies th=
at recently closed SPAC mergers, reinvigorating some of the meme-stock exci=
tement that helped make such deals popular early in the year.The latest spe=
cial-purpose-acquisition-company excitement focuses on firms like cybersecu=
rity firm IronNet Inc. that suffered significant investor withdrawals ahead=
 of going public by closing SPAC mergers. High withdrawals leave the compan=
ies going public with less cash to put into their businesses and can make i=
t harder for them to meet the growth projections they made as part of the d=
eals with so-called blank-check companies.=C2=A0That drop in cash proceeds =
is why high withdrawals have historically sunk share prices. But lately, la=
rge withdrawals are attracting day traders, making some stocks trend on soc=
ial-media platforms and fueling share-price gains.Basically you can get the=
 whole meme-stock playbook but in=C2=A0concentrated form:A number of factor=
s are boosting shares after SPAC deals close, investors say. Large investor=
 withdrawals reduce the number of shares available to trade, also known as =
a stock=E2=80=99s float. That scarcity means it doesn=E2=80=99t take much t=
o swing the stock. Companies that go public via SPACs are also popular targ=
ets for short sellers, who wager on stock-price declines, a trend that can =
attract day traders hoping to =E2=80=9Csqueeze=E2=80=9D the professionals b=
y bidding up the shares.Short sellers borrow shares, sell them, then aim to=
 buy them back at lower prices. A low float also makes it challenging for t=
raders to borrow shares. That means it can be relatively easy to force thos=
e trying to wager on share-price declines to limit losses by buying, analys=
ts say, adding fuel to rallies.=C2=A0Rising trading in options=E2=80=94whic=
h give the holder the right to buy a stock at a certain price in the future=
=E2=80=94is also playing a role. Market makers that sell options often hedg=
e by buying shares of the underlying stock, a force that can help amplify s=
hare-price gains.=C2=A0If you have a SPAC with 90% withdrawals that closes =
its merger anyway, what you are effectively doing is concentrating the trad=
ing of that company entirely in the hands of retail meme-stock zealots. Wei=
rd stuff happens.

   =20

I think it=E2=80=99s fair to say that people don=E2=80=99t trust market mak=
ers. The idea that one firm buys a thing if you want to sell it, and sells =
it if you want to buy it, and=C2=A0tells you what the price is=C2=A0=E2=80=
=94=C2=A0it seems like too much power, you know? Too monopolistic? Classica=
lly in, say, the stock and bond markets, there are lots of market makers, f=
iercely competing with one another, and none of them seems particularly mon=
opolistic. But in the modern Reddit-driven retail stock market, retail inve=
stors don=E2=80=99t really get a choice of market maker, and while the busi=
ness of market making for retail customers remains fiercely competitive, it=
 is competitive in  a strange way that doesn=E2=80=99t necessarily make tho=
se investors feel good. Also the big market makers are big and their founde=
rs are rich and so forth.And so there is a real efflorescence of market-mak=
er conspiracy theories, most of which I don=E2=80=99t talk about around her=
e, and I=E2=80=99m not about to start now.On the other hand, the way that b=
uying and selling houses traditionally works, in the U.S., is that there is=
 not a market maker. There is no liquidity provider. If you want to sell yo=
ur house, you put up a sign saying =E2=80=9Chouse for sale,=E2=80=9D and if=
 you want to buy a house you drive around looking for those signs, and if a=
 buyer meets a seller they negotiate a bilateral deal between themselves. I=
 mean, there=E2=80=99s more to it than that =E2=80=94=C2=A0the buyer and se=
ller can and usually do hire agents to help them look for each other; the =
=E2=80=9Cfor sale=E2=80=9D signs are mostly online and searchable these day=
s =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it is an essentially bilateral market; generally when =
a house is sold, the person who lived in it for a while sells it to another=
 person who plans to live in it. You don=E2=80=99t normally sell your house=
 to a market maker who sells it to someone else five minutes later.But you =
could. You could imagine a company that stood ready to provide liquidity in=
 the housing market. If you wanted to sell your house, it would bid you a p=
rice for your house. If you wanted to buy a house, it would have a bunch of=
 houses available and offer them to you at a price. It would aim to profit =
from the spread between its buying and selling prices, which means of cours=
e that if you sold a house to this market maker it would pay you=C2=A0less=
=C2=A0than a =E2=80=9Creal=E2=80=9D buyer would pay. (And then it would tur=
n around and sell the house to that =E2=80=9Creal=E2=80=9D buyer at the pri=
ce she would pay.) You lose the spread, but you gain liquidity and immediac=
y: If you want to sell a house, the market maker will buy it from you in ca=
sh that day, which you may find more appealing than putting out a sign and =
hoping a real buyer shows up.=C2=A0For this to work, the market maker would=
 need to do a lot of trades; it would need to buy and sell a lot of houses.=
 It would get a lot of information from all this trading, and develop a goo=
d sense of what houses are worth. Eventually everyone might adapt their beh=
avior to the existence of the market maker: Instead of driving around looki=
ng for for-sale signs, buyers would just go to the market maker to buy a ho=
use, so sellers would not attract buyers with their for-sale signs and woul=
d have no one to sell to but the market maker. It might become a bit of a m=
onopolist, at least until competing well-capitalized well-informed market m=
akers could get into the market.=C2=A0It might take on, as market makers do=
, an almost official role. If it quoted you a price on your house, you migh=
t think of that not as =E2=80=9Chere is what one potential buyer is bidding=
 for my house=E2=80=9D but rather =E2=80=9Cthis is what the market maker sa=
ys my house is worth=E2=80=9D; if the price it quotes is lower than you hop=
ed for, you will think not =E2=80=9Cwell I=E2=80=99d better sell to a highe=
r bidder=E2=80=9D but =E2=80=9Cthis market maker is being predatory and unf=
air; it is pricing my house too low.=E2=80=9DAnyway is Zillow front-running=
 your house search? Is Zillow painting the tape to push up the price of hou=
ses? Is Zillow using retail home-buying data to get an informational advant=
age and trade homes ahead of retail home-buying patterns? I don=E2=80=99t k=
now, probably a little bit, why not?=C2=A0But to the extent the market stru=
cture of U.S. real estate becomes more market-maker-driven, you=E2=80=99re =
going to  hear a lot more of these theories:=C2=A0Runaway home prices are f=
ueling angst on social media over the role of big corporations in the U.S. =
housing market.Zillow Group Inc. became the latest company to get enmeshed =
in internet drama when Sean Gotcher =E2=80=94 a real estate agent in Las Ve=
gas =E2=80=94 posted a TikTok video that said an unnamed company was pullin=
g off a convoluted scheme to manipulate housing prices in his home market. =
=E2=80=A6In the hypothetical he lays out in the video, the company uses its=
 cash to amass an inventory of 30 homes in a given neighborhood, all for on=
e price: $300,000. Then the company buys an additional home for $340,000 in=
 a bid to drive up the value of the first 30 homes.=C2=A0This is a devious =
and possibly brilliant idea, but probably not what Zillow is up to.A much m=
ore likely explanation is that Zillow sometimes overpays for homes, because=
 the market has been really hot, and at times prices in certain markets hav=
e been moving so fast that it=E2=80=99s hard to know what a property is wor=
th.Zillow said in a statement that it pays market value for every home it b=
uys.Yes look right if Zillow can buy a bunch of houses for $300,000 and sel=
l them at $340,000 it should do that, that=E2=80=99s a good spread, why wou=
ldn=E2=80=99t it do that?=C2=A0

The  weird story of Bluestone Resources Inc. is that it took out $850 milli=
on of, like =E2=80=A6=C2=A0payday loans? =E2=80=A6=C2=A0from Greensill Capi=
tal. Greensill was in the business of making short-term loans secured by co=
mpanies=E2=80=99 accounts receivable, and it did that for Bluestone, and th=
en Bluestone wanted more money, and Greensill and Bluestone just agreed tha=
t Greensill would lend it more money =E2=80=94=C2=A0unsecured, long term =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0and sort of=C2=A0pretend=C2=A0that it was short-term lending=
 secured by accounts receivable. Specifically the lending documents provide=
d that the loans were secured by receivables, but they were =E2=80=9Cprospe=
ctive receivables=E2=80=9D from =E2=80=9Cprospective customers.=E2=80=9D An=
d they provided that the loans were=C2=A0short-term loans, but each time th=
ey came due Bluestone would pay some interest and Greensill would roll them=
 over again.And then Greensill imploded, and Greensill=E2=80=99s administra=
tors came to Bluestone and were like =E2=80=9Chey you=E2=80=99ve got $850 m=
illion of short-term loans due, hand it over,=E2=80=9D and Bluestone was li=
ke =E2=80=9Cno we don=E2=80=99t have that kind of money we thought these lo=
ans would keep rolling over for years,=E2=80=9D and Greensill=E2=80=99s adm=
inistrators were like =E2=80=9Cah but surely we will just collect the $850 =
million of accounts receivable securing those loans=E2=80=9D and Bluestone =
was like =E2=80=9Coh no those were fake receivables=E2=80=9D and Greensill=
=E2=80=99s administrators were like =E2=80=9Cwait fake receivables?=E2=80=
=9D and Bluestone was like =E2=80=9Cyou must be new here.=E2=80=9D[2] And B=
luestone ended up suing Greensill to try to get a court to agree that the s=
hort-term loans were actually long-term loans and that it should have more =
time to pay them back, which was honestly kind of a long shot.=C2=A0It does=
n=E2=80=99t seem to be going great, but here=E2=80=99s this:West Virginia G=
ov. Jim Justice has offered a $300 million payment and half the value of hi=
s family coal businesses to settle loans from the now-defunct Greensill Cap=
ital, according to people familiar with the deliberations.The proposal is p=
art of talks between the governor=E2=80=99s family business, Bluestone Reso=
urces Inc., and Credit Suisse Group AG, which manages investment funds that=
 were Greensill=E2=80=99s main source of cash for its lending business. =E2=
=80=A6In the talks with Credit Suisse, Mr. Justice=E2=80=99s companies have=
 offered to pay Credit Suisse $300 million, the people said. It would sourc=
e the money by refinancing a chunk of the existing loans with a third-party=
 lender. It couldn=E2=80=99t be learned who the lender for the transaction =
would be.Mr. Justice would also pledge to pay half of the proceeds to Credi=
t Suisse of a sale or initial public offering of Bluestone, the family comp=
any that operates coal mines and that took the loans. Any sale would have t=
o take into account the company=E2=80=99s debt load, the people said.Credit=
 Suisse has said in notices to investors that Bluestone owes it nearly $700=
 million.=C2=A0So if you put money into Credit Suisse=E2=80=99s safe short-=
term funds backed by Greensill=E2=80=99s receivables financing for Blueston=
e, you get back like =E2=80=A6=C2=A042 cents on the dollar plus half a coal=
 company? That doesn=E2=80=99t especially seem like what you=E2=80=99d want=
.

Schematically, Activision Blizzard Inc. is in three sorts of legal trouble =
for alleged sexual harassment and gender discrimination:=09It is in trouble=
 with the U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission, which protects work=
ers from discrimination and has the power to sue Activision to punish haras=
sment and compensate victims.=09It is being  sued by the California Departm=
ent of Fair Employment and Housing for similar reasons.=C2=A0=09It is being=
  sued by shareholders because its stock went down after Items 1 and 2 beca=
me public, and if a public company does sexual harassment that is securitie=
s fraud.=09It is being  investigated by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Co=
mmission, which protects shareholders of U.S. public companies, and see Ite=
m 3.Yesterday Activision settled with the EEOC for $18 million. The press r=
elease says that the $18 million will be used =E2=80=9Cto compensate and ma=
ke amends to eligible claimants,=E2=80=9D with any excess going to charitie=
s and corporate diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives. So the victims=
 of sexual harassment and discrimination at Activision will get some compen=
sation, capped at $18 million.That=E2=80=99s Item 1. Item 2 is in process. =
We are still in the early stages of Items 3 and 4 =E2=80=94=C2=A0the lawsui=
ts in Item 3 got going last month, and the SEC investigation in Item 4 is i=
n the early-rumors stage =E2=80=94=C2=A0but I guess my question is, will th=
ey settle for more or less than $18 million? In cases of corporate sexual h=
arassment, do the people who are sexually harassed get more or less compens=
ation than the shareholders? I don=E2=80=99t know the answer but I have a g=
uess! Activision has lost about $12 billion of market capitalization since =
the California DFEH sued in July.=C2=A0

<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=

<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Wells Fargo FX</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There is at this point a standard way =
for a bank to rip off its foreign-exchange customers. It goes like this. A =
customer =E2=80=94=C2=A0a corporate client, small business, small bank, wha=
tever =E2=80=94=C2=A0wants to use a bank for regular foreign-exchange trans=
actions; its customers pay in euros and it wants dollars, or it has dollars=
 and its suppliers want to be paid in euros. The bank says, sure, we will c=
hange dollars into euros for you whenever you like, and charge you 25 pips.=
 If you want euros and we can buy them for $1.1600, we=E2=80=99ll sell them=
 to you for $1.1625. The customer and the bank agree to this pricing.</p><p=
 style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Then the customer comes to the bank and says =E2=
=80=9CI=E2=80=99d like 10 million euros please.=E2=80=9D The bank says, sur=
e, we=E2=80=99ll have them by the end of the day. At the end of the day the=
 bank looks at the trading range of the euro. Say the euro traded as low as=
 $1.1685 and as high as $1.1732 that day. The bank will just=C2=A0<em>pick =
the highest price</em>, and charge the customer that, plus the (agreed) mar=
kup. So the customer will pay $1.1732 (the high price) plus $0.0025 (the ma=
rkup), or $1.1757 total, or $11,757,000 for its 10 million euros. The custo=
mer thinks the bank made $25,000 (the 25-pip markup on 10 million euros). O=
f course the bank didn=E2=80=99t actually pay the highest price. If it boug=
ht the euros at, say, $1.1711, then it made $46,000 on the trade.[1] It has=
 almost doubled its fee without telling the customer.</p><p style=3D"margin=
: 16px 0;">This is bad! At least, it is bad if the bank has said things to =
the client like =E2=80=9Cwe will try to get you the best price=E2=80=9D rat=
her than =E2=80=9Cwe will try to get you the worst price, to maximize our m=
arkup.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0That is common, and so banks have gotten in trouble fo=
r it. (We <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25172656=
.276397/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMTUtMDM=
tMTkvYmFuay1vZi1uZXcteW9yay1tZWxsb24tcy1iZXN0LWZ4LWV4ZWN1dGlvbi13YXMtcHJldH=
R5LWJhZA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB027b7a25" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscop=
e=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; =
border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style=
: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> talked about</a> Bank of New York Mellon=
 getting in trouble for it in 2015.) It=E2=80=99s a thing, it happens, it=
=E2=80=99s bad, it=E2=80=99s a standard way to get in trouble.</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Once you are doing it, though =E2=80=A6=C2=A0I mean, t=
here are two ways to think about this trade. One is =E2=80=9Cwell, we=C2=A0=
<em>could have=C2=A0</em>bought euros at the highest price, so we=E2=80=99r=
e not being particularly dishonest by charging them the highest price.=E2=
=80=9D This does not seem especially compelling. The other is =E2=80=9Cwell=
, the customer=C2=A0<em>isn=E2=80=99t paying attention</em>, so we might as=
 well charge them the highest price. We are experts in the FX market, and t=
hey aren=E2=80=99t, so let=E2=80=99s use our expertise to gouge them.=E2=80=
=9D=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If that=E2=80=99s how you think a=
bout the trade, it seems silly to limit yourself to the highest trading pri=
ce of the day. If the client is paying a little bit of attention, you might=
 fool her by charging the highest price of the day. But if she=E2=80=99s no=
t paying attention at all, why not charge her more? Why not just=C2=A0<em>m=
ake up=C2=A0</em>a number and see what happens?=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin=
: 16px 0;">Today federal prosecutors <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/25172656.276397/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuanVzdGljZS5nb3YvdXNhby1z=
ZG55L3ByL21hbmhhdHRhbi11cy1hdHRvcm5leS1hbm5vdW5jZXMtNzI2LW1pbGxpb24tc2V0dGx=
lbWVudC1mcmF1ZC1sYXdzdWl0LWFnYWluc3Qtd2VsbHM/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBbc22c=
8a2" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000=
; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom=
-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">annou=
nced a $72.6 million settlement</a> with Wells Fargo Bank N.A. for overchar=
ging business FX customers. Wells Fargo definitely did the standard, highes=
t-price-of-the-day thing:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Furth=
ermore, rather than charging the agreed-upon fixed spread to the FX market =
rate at the time the outgoing wire was converted, FX sales specialists woul=
d select the best rate for the Bank and worst rate for the customer from th=
e FX price fluctuations from the beginning of the trading day until the tim=
e of the transaction. =C2=A0This practice was referred to internally as =E2=
=80=9CRange of Day=E2=80=9D Pricing.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">And here is how=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.=
com/click/25172656.276397/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuanVzdGljZS5nb3YvdXNhby1zZG55L3ByZ=
XNzLXJlbGVhc2UvZmlsZS8xNDM2ODQxL2Rvd25sb2Fk/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB29c00e=
b8" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000;=
 text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-=
color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">the co=
mplaint</a>=C2=A0describes Wells Fargo=E2=80=99s pricing when it converted =
incoming wire transfers for customers:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 1=
6px 0;">Because Wells Fargo generally did not provide immediate notice to c=
ustomers when they received incoming wires, known as BSwifts, Wells Fargo's=
 FX sales=C2=A0specialists took advantage of this time delay to charge high=
er spreads than the Bank had represented it would.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"mar=
gin: 16px 0;">One method used by FX sales specialists to extract large sale=
s margins on BSwift transactions was to price them based on the range of th=
e day. This practice allowed FX sales=C2=A0specialists to generate even lar=
ger spreads on incoming wire transactions than on outgoing wires. With outg=
oing wires, FX sales specialists could select the best price for the Bank a=
nd the worst price for the customer only from the beginning of the trading =
day up until the time of the transaction. However, with BSwift wires, becau=
se the customers generally did not know when=C2=A0the funds arrived in thei=
r accounts or when they were converted, the FX sales specialist could wait =
until the end of the day and cherry-pick the best rate for the Bank and the=
 worst rate for the customer from the entire trading day.</p><p style=3D"ma=
rgin: 16px 0;">In a written instant message to a colleague, an FX sales spe=
cialist analogized this fraudulent scheme to getting candy from a pinata, r=
eferring to the Bank's pricing of BSwift wire transfers as the "BSWIFT pina=
ta."=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Fine sure right. Bu=
t that is just a jumping-off point; once you=E2=80=99re making up prices, w=
hy not, you know, really make up prices?=C2=A0</p><blockquote><p style=3D"m=
argin: 16px 0;">For some customers, FX sales specialists also used what the=
y internally called the "big figure trick" or the "transposition error game=
" to increase the FX sales margin and their profits. To carry out this sche=
me, the FX specialist would intentionally transpose the digits in the price=
 of the transactions in a way that would result in the customers paying Wel=
ls Fargo a higher spread or sales margin.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">F=
or example, hypothetically, if the actual correct price to purchase a Euro =
was 1.0123 dollars, an FX sales specialist would use the big figure trick t=
o switch the price to 1.0213 dollars, thus taking more spread (in this exam=
ple, an additional 89 basis points) from the customer.</p><p style=3D"margi=
n: 16px 0;">If the customer actually caught the error, the FX sales special=
ist would falsely claim that it was simply a mistake.</p><p style=3D"margin=
: 16px 0;">One FX sales specialist explained in an internal communication w=
ith colleagues: "You can play the transposition error game if you get calle=
d out." Another FX sales specialist noted to a colleague regarding a previo=
us transaction that a customer "didn't flinch at the big fig the other day.=
 Want to take a bit more?"</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Man=
. I love writing about the complex stratagems that the world=E2=80=99s bigg=
est and most sophisticated financial institutions use to give themselves an=
 edge. My favorite remains =E2=80=9Cif someone sends you money by accident,=
 <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25172656.276397/a=
HR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDItMTcvY2l0=
aS1jYW4tdC1oYXZlLWl0cy05MDAtbWlsbGlvbi1iYWNr/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB2319f=
55a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000=
; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom=
-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> keep=
 it</a>,=E2=80=9D but this one is pretty good too. =E2=80=9CSwitch the digi=
ts in a price to make it higher, and hope the customer doesn=E2=80=99t noti=
ce,=E2=80=9D that=E2=80=99s high finance right there.</p><table class=3D"li=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=

5px;">SPAC SPAC SPAC</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">What=C2=A0<em>is=C2=A0</em>a special p=
urpose acquisition company? Here is the story that I would have told you a =
year ago, at the height of the SPAC boom. A SPAC is a pool of money that a =

ompany. When she finds a promising private company, the sponsor says, look,=
 I=E2=80=99ve got $200 million in my pot,=C2=A0and=C2=A0the pot is a public=
ly listed stock; if you merge with my pot, you will get the money to run yo=
ur business, and you will have a public listing.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16p=
x 0;">The private company will find this appealing. For one thing, it is a =
faster and easier route to a public listing than a traditional initial publ=

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I think it=E2=80=99s fair to say that =
people don=E2=80=99t trust market makers. The idea that one firm buys a thi=
ng if you want to sell it, and sells it if you want to buy it, and=C2=A0<em=
>tells you what the price is=C2=A0</em>=E2=80=94=C2=A0it seems like too muc=

d markets, there are lots of market makers, fiercely competing with one ano=
ther, and none of them seems particularly monopolistic. But in the modern R=
eddit-driven retail stock market, retail investors <a href=3D"https://link.=

" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-=
width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backg=
round-color: #ccc;">don=E2=80=99t really get a choice of market maker</a>, =
and while the business of market making for retail customers remains fierce=
ly competitive, it is competitive in <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/25172656.276397/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGlu=
aW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDktMjMvZGFyay1wb29sLXNvbGQtc29tZS1vcmRlci1mbG93/60=

ope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; ba=
ckground-color: #ccc;"> a strange way</a> that doesn=E2=80=99t necessarily =
make those investors feel good. Also the big market makers are big and thei=
r founders are rich and so forth.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so th=
ere is a real efflorescence of market-maker conspiracy theories, most of wh=
ich I don=E2=80=99t talk about around here, and I=E2=80=99m not about to st=
art now.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On the other hand, the way that bu=
ying and selling houses traditionally works, in the U.S., is that there is =
not a market maker. There is no liquidity provider. If you want to sell you=
r house, you put up a sign saying =E2=80=9Chouse for sale,=E2=80=9D and if =
you want to buy a house you drive around looking for those signs, and if a =
buyer meets a seller they negotiate a bilateral deal between themselves. I =
mean, there=E2=80=99s more to it than that =E2=80=94=C2=A0the buyer and sel=
ler can and usually do hire agents to help them look for each other; the =
=E2=80=9Cfor sale=E2=80=9D signs are mostly online and searchable these day=
s =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it is an essentially bilateral market; generally when =
a house is sold, the person who lived in it for a while sells it to another=
 person who plans to live in it. You don=E2=80=99t normally sell your house=
 to a market maker who sells it to someone else five minutes later.</p><p s=
tyle=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But you could. You could imagine a company that st=
ood ready to provide liquidity in the housing market. If you wanted to sell=
 your house, it would bid you a price for your house. If you wanted to buy =
a house, it would have a bunch of houses available and offer them to you at=
 a price. It would aim to profit from the spread between its buying and sel=
ling prices, which means of course that if you sold a house to this market =
maker it would pay you=C2=A0<em>less=C2=A0</em>than a =E2=80=9Creal=E2=80=
=9D buyer would pay. (And then it would turn around and sell the house to t=
hat =E2=80=9Creal=E2=80=9D buyer at the price she would pay.) You lose the =
spread, but you gain liquidity and immediacy: If you want to sell a house, =
the market maker will buy it from you in cash that day, which you may find =
more appealing than putting out a sign and hoping a real buyer shows up.=C2=
=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">For this to work, the market maker woul=
d need to do a lot of trades; it would need to buy and sell a lot of houses=
. It would get a lot of information from all this trading, and develop a go=
od sense of what houses are worth. Eventually everyone might adapt their be=
havior to the existence of the market maker: Instead of driving around look=
ing for for-sale signs, buyers would just go to the market maker to buy a h=
ouse, so sellers would not attract buyers with their for-sale signs and wou=
ld have no one to sell to but the market maker. It might become a bit of a =
monopolist, at least until competing well-capitalized well-informed market =
makers could get into the market.=C2=A0It might take on, as market makers d=
o, an almost official role. If it quoted you a price on your house, you mig=
ht think of that not as =E2=80=9Chere is what one potential buyer is biddin=
g for my house=E2=80=9D but rather =E2=80=9Cthis is what the market maker s=
ays my house is worth=E2=80=9D; if the price it quotes is lower than you ho=
ped for, you will think not =E2=80=9Cwell I=E2=80=99d better sell to a high=
